activities_projects.title: Poem Understanding Activity
activities_projects.objective: Understand the central idea of the poem
steps: Read the poem carefully
Identify the problem the poet describes
Write why the poet asks the wind to come softly
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Understands the basic concept and message of the poem

activities_projects.title: Stanza Meaning Activity
activities_projects.objective: Analyze the meaning of different parts of the poem
steps: Divide the poem into two main parts
Write the meaning of each part
Discuss how the tone changes in the poem

activities_projects.title: Imagery Identification
activities_projects.objective: Recognize imagery used in the poem
steps: Find lines that describe destruction caused by wind
Write how the poet describes houses and doors
Explain how imagery helps readers imagine the scene

activities_projects.title: Personification Activity
steps: Identify how the poet treats the wind like a person
Write two examples from the poem
Explain the effect of personification

activities_projects.title: Cause and Effect Chart
steps: Draw two columns named 'Cause' and 'Effect'
Write what the wind does in the first column
Write its effects on houses, books, and people in the second column

activities_projects.title: Message Interpretation
activities_projects.objective: Understand the deeper message of the poem
steps: Read the lines about strong fires and weak fires
Explain what these lines symbolize
Write the lesson the poet wants to give

activities_projects.title: Group Discussion
steps: Discuss why the poet calls wind a friend
Share different opinions in the group
Write the final conclusion of the discussion

activities_projects.title: Real Life Connection
activities_projects.objective: Relate the poem to real-life situations
steps: Think about challenges people face in life
Write how strong people handle difficulties
Relate it to the message of the poem

activities_projects.title: Poetic Tone Activity
activities_projects.objective: Identify the tone of the poem
steps: Read the poem carefully
Write the tone used by the poet (warning, advice, etc.)
Explain your answer with lines from the poem

activities_projects.title: Creative Response Activity
steps: Imagine you are talking to the wind
Write 6–7 lines giving advice to the wind
Share your ideas in class
